hub.title: IBD Patient Guides — Crohn's, Colitis & Daily Life | IBDPal
hub.description: Searchable patient guides for Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis: diet, flares, support groups, symptom tracking, visit prep, and more. Free education from ibdpal.org.
hub.intro: Each guide targets a common search topic—foods to eat during a flare, finding support near you, tracking symptoms, and more. Education only; always work with your IBD care team.

pages.title: What Should I Eat with Crohn's or Colitis? | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Practical IBD diet guidance: foods many people tolerate in remission vs. during flares, hydration, and when to see a dietitian—education only, not medical advice.
pages.h1: What should I eat with Crohn's or colitis?
pages.intro: There is no single diet for inflammatory bowel disease. Triggers differ by person and disease activity. This guide summarizes patterns many patients discuss with their gastroenterologist or IBD dietitian.

sections.heading: During remission
paragraphs: A balanced pattern with fruits, vegetables, lean protein, and whole grains is a common goal when symptoms are quiet.
Some people reintroduce higher-fiber foods slowly with their team's guidance.

sections.heading: During a flare
paragraphs: Softer, lower-fiber choices—bananas, white rice, applesauce, broths, lean protein—are often easier to tolerate temporarily.
Hydration matters when diarrhea or reduced intake is present.

tips: Keep a simple food and symptom log
Introduce one new food at a time
Ask about vitamin D, iron, and B12 labs

related.label: Best foods during a Crohn's flare (article)

faq.q: Is there one best diet for Crohn's disease?
faq.a: No. Individual triggers and disease activity vary. Work with your IBD team for a plan that fits you.
Not always. Many people reduce fiber during active symptoms and expand variety in remission—with clinician guidance.

pages.title: Foods to Eat During a Crohn's Flare | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Gentle food ideas, hydration, and common triggers during an active Crohn's flare. Educational guide—not a substitute for your gastroenterologist.
pages.h1: Foods to eat during a Crohn's flare
pages.intro: When inflammation is active, many people focus on easy-to-digest meals and fluids. These are general patterns—not rules that apply to everyone.

sections.heading: Often easier to tolerate
paragraphs: Soft fruits (bananas, applesauce), refined grains (white rice, plain pasta), broth-based soups, eggs, tofu, and lean poultry or fish are frequent choices in patient education materials.

sections.heading: Hydration
paragraphs: Water, oral rehydration solutions, broth, and herbal teas may help when fluid losses are higher. Limit caffeine and alcohol if they worsen symptoms.

tips: Eat smaller, more frequent meals if large portions trigger discomfort
Avoid known personal triggers even if they are healthy for others

related.label: Full article: best foods during a flare

faq.q: Can diet stop a Crohn's flare?
faq.a: Diet alone does not treat inflammation. Medication and medical care from your IBD team are essential; food choices may reduce irritation while you recover.

pages.title: Ulcerative Colitis Diet & Foods | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: UC nutrition basics: remission vs. flare patterns, hydration, and tracking triggers. Educational guide for ulcerative colitis patients and caregivers.
pages.intro: Ulcerative colitis primarily affects the colon. Nutrition goals often include maintaining weight, correcting deficiencies, and identifying foods that worsen symptoms for you personally.

sections.heading: Remission goals
paragraphs: A varied, nutrient-dense pattern supports long-term health when tolerated.
Some patients work with an IBD dietitian on reintroducing fiber and plant foods.

sections.heading: Active symptoms
paragraphs: Lower-fiber, low-residue patterns are sometimes used short term.
See our guide on UC trigger foods for common items patients monitor.

related.label: Foods that may trigger UC symptoms
What should I eat with IBD?

faq.q: Is ulcerative colitis diet the same as Crohn's?
faq.a: Principles overlap, but location of disease and personal triggers differ. Follow guidance tailored to your diagnosis.

pages.title: Crohn's Disease Diet & Nutrition | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: How nutrition fits into Crohn's disease care: deficiencies, flare vs. remission eating, and tools to spot patterns. Education only.
pages.intro: Crohn's can affect any part of the digestive tract, so nutritional needs vary widely. Tracking intake and symptoms helps you bring clearer data to clinic visits.

sections.heading: Why nutrition matters
paragraphs: Malnutrition, anemia, and vitamin deficiencies are common in IBD.
Diet does not replace medication, but it supports energy, growth (in youth), and recovery.

sections.heading: Using data at appointments
paragraphs: A food and symptom log—or IBDPal export—helps your team see patterns between visits.

pages.title: Low-Residue Diet for IBD | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: When a low-residue or low-fiber pattern may be discussed during IBD flares, example foods, and how long to use it—with your care team's input.
pages.intro: A low-residue pattern limits fiber and tough-to-digest foods for a short period for some patients during active symptoms. It is not meant to be permanent without medical supervision.

sections.heading: Common inclusions
paragraphs: Refined grains, peeled cooked vegetables, tender meats, eggs, and smooth dairy (if lactose is tolerated) appear in many patient handouts.

sections.heading: Transition back
paragraphs: As symptoms improve, teams often help reintroduce fiber gradually to support long-term gut health.

pages.title: Hydration & Fluids for IBD | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Why dehydration is common in IBD, drink choices during flares, and when to call your care team. Educational hydration guide.
pages.intro: Diarrhea, vomiting, and reduced oral intake increase dehydration risk in Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis.

sections.heading: Practical tips
paragraphs: Sip fluids throughout the day; consider electrolyte drinks when losses are high.
Broths and oral rehydration solutions are common suggestions in patient education.

faq.a: Dizziness, very dark urine, rapid heartbeat, or inability to keep fluids down warrant urgent medical contact—not only self-care at home.

pages.title: Crohn's & Colitis Support Groups | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Find in-person and virtual IBD support groups by state, plus national helplines and the IBDPal community map.
pages.intro: Peer support complements medical care. National finders and local chapters help you connect with others who understand IBD daily life.

sections.heading: National starting points
paragraphs: The Crohn's & Colitis Foundation offers chapter programs and a support group locator.
ImproveCareNow connects pediatric IBD families with care centers and community resources.

faq.a: No. They provide peer connection and education. Treatment decisions stay with your clinicians.

pages.title: IBD Support Near Me — Local Resources | IBDPal
pages.description: Find IBD and Crohn's support near you: state chapters, support groups, and major centers on IBDPal's U.S. community map.
pages.intro: Local chapters, hospital programs, and virtual groups vary by state. Start with national finders, then narrow by your location.

sections.heading: Use the community map
paragraphs: IBDPal's Community tab lists state-level chapters, helplines, and pediatric networks—with detailed listings for North Carolina and national fallbacks for all states.

pages.title: Pediatric Crohn's & Colitis Help | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Resources for children and teens with IBD: ImproveCareNow, GIKids, school planning, and family tracking with IBDPal.
pages.intro: Families need team-based care, school support, and trustworthy education networks—not generic adult advice.

sections.heading: Trusted networks
paragraphs: ImproveCareNow and GIKids are widely used for pediatric IBD education and care center discovery.
IBDPal's pediatric hub links these resources with visit-prep and tracking tools.

related.label: High school with Crohn's or colitis
Living with IBD as a family

pages.title: Newly Diagnosed with Crohn's or Colitis | IBDPal
pages.description: First steps after an IBD diagnosis: questions for your GI, support links, visit prep, and free tracking tools.
pages.h1: Newly diagnosed with Crohn's or colitis
pages.intro: A new diagnosis is overwhelming. Focus on understanding your type of IBD, building your care team, and knowing who to call for support—not emergency medical advice online.

sections.heading: Questions for your first visits
paragraphs: What type of IBD do I have and where is it active?
What are my treatment options and monitoring plan?
Who do I contact after hours for worsening symptoms?

pages.title: Crohn's Flare — What to Do | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Calm first steps during a Crohn's flare: when to call your team, hydration, diet adjustments, and what not to delay.
pages.intro: A flare means worsening inflammation or symptoms. Medical treatment decisions belong with your IBD team; this guide outlines common self-care topics patients discuss in clinic.

sections.heading: Contact your team if
paragraphs: Fever, severe pain, blood in stool, inability to eat or drink, or symptoms very different from your baseline warrant prompt outreach.

pages.title: Ulcerative Colitis Flare Management | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: UC flare basics: symptoms to report, hydration, diet patterns, and clinic communication. Education only.
pages.intro: Colitis flares often involve increased bowel frequency, urgency, blood, or fatigue. Early communication with your gastroenterologist can prevent complications.

sections.heading: Document for your visit
paragraphs: Stool frequency, blood, pain level, weight change, and medications taken help your team adjust treatment quickly.

pages.title: Track IBD Symptoms & Food | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Why logging meals and symptoms helps IBD care, what to record, and free tracking with the IBDPal iOS app.
pages.intro: Patterns between diet, stress, sleep, and symptoms are hard to remember months later. A simple log improves visit conversations.

sections.heading: What to log
paragraphs: Meals, bowel symptoms, pain, energy, medications, and sleep are common fields patients track.
Photos or notes about restaurant meals can help recall triggers.

pages.title: IBD Nutrition Tracking App — IBDPal | Guide
pages.description: Free iOS app for IBD nutrition, symptom, and flare tracking with visit-prep exports. MediVue nonprofit—education only.
pages.intro: IBDPal helps patients log meals, micronutrients, symptoms, and medications—then export summaries for gastroenterology visits.

sections.heading: Features patients use
paragraphs: Daily nutrition scoring, symptom trends, medication reminders, and PDF/CSV visit exports.
Community features connect you with others living with IBD.

pages.title: Prepare for Your Crohn's / IBD Doctor Visit | IBDPal
pages.description: Printable IBD visit checklist: symptoms, meds, questions, and trends to share with your gastroenterologist.
pages.h1: Prepare for your Crohn's or IBD doctor visit
pages.intro: Short appointments go further when you arrive with organized notes, medication lists, and clear questions.

sections.heading: Bring or export
paragraphs: Symptom trends, weight changes, medication adherence, and nutrition concerns.
IBDPal can export a visit summary from your logs.

pages.title: Biologics for Crohn's & Colitis — Overview | IBDPal
pages.description: Patient-friendly overview of biologic therapies in IBD: questions for your team, insurance topics, and education links.
pages.h1: Biologics for Crohn's and colitis — overview
pages.intro: Biologics are advanced therapies used for many people with moderate-to-severe IBD. Only your prescriber can say if they are appropriate for you.

sections.heading: Questions patients ask
paragraphs: How will we know if it's working?
What are infusion vs. injection options?
How do we handle insurance or prior authorization?

pages.title: Travel With IBD — Planning Guide | IBDPal
pages.description: Travel tips for Crohn's and colitis: medications, restrooms, food planning, and flare preparedness.
pages.intro: Travel is possible with planning: medication supply, restroom access, travel insurance, and a flare plan with your team.

pages.title: IBD Workplace & School Rights | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Overview of workplace and school accommodations for Crohn's and colitis: ADA, 504 plans, and documentation tips.
pages.h1: Workplace and school rights with IBD
pages.intro: Many students and employees benefit from documented accommodations for bathroom access, absences, and medication schedules.

pages.title: Living With an Ostomy & IBD | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Ostomy basics for IBD patients: peer support, supplies, and emotional adjustment resources.
pages.h1: Living with an ostomy and IBD
pages.intro: An ostomy can be part of treatment for some people with Crohn's or colitis. Peer networks and clinicians help with practical and emotional adjustment.

pages.title: Stress, Anxiety & IBD | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: How stress and mood interact with IBD symptoms, coping strategies, and when to seek mental health support.
pages.intro: Chronic illness affects mental health. Stress does not cause IBD, but it can worsen how you feel day to day alongside physical symptoms.

pages.title: Sleep & Rest During IBD Flares | IBDPal Guide
pages.description: Sleep disruption and fatigue during IBD flares: practical rest tips and when to discuss symptoms with your team.
pages.h1: Sleep and rest during IBD flares
pages.intro: Nighttime symptoms, pain, and anxiety commonly disrupt sleep during flares. Rest supports recovery but does not replace medical treatment.

pages.title: Partner & Caregiver Guide for IBD | IBDPal
pages.description: How partners and caregivers can support someone with Crohn's or colitis without taking over medical decisions.
pages.h1: Partners and caregivers: supporting someone with IBD
pages.intro: Caregivers provide practical and emotional support. Clear boundaries and open communication with the medical team help everyone.

pages.title: Crohn's Food Triggers — What Patients Track | IBDPal
pages.description: Common food triggers patients discuss with their IBD team—individual results vary. Track yours with a food log.
pages.intro: Trigger foods are personal. Spicy, high-fat, high-fiber, alcohol, and lactose are frequent discussion points—not universal rules.

sections.heading: Track instead of guess
paragraphs: A structured log beats eliminating long food lists without data.
IBDPal helps correlate meals with symptom patterns over time.

pages.title: IBD Helpline & Help Center Numbers | IBDPal
pages.description: National IBD helplines and help centers—including the Crohn's & Colitis Foundation IBD Help Center—for education and referrals.
pages.h1: IBD helpline and help center numbers
pages.intro: Helplines provide information and program referrals—not emergency medical care. For urgent symptoms, contact your clinician or emergency services.

sections.heading: Crohn's & Colitis Foundation
paragraphs: IBD Help Center: 888-MY-GUT-PAIN (888-694-8872) for education and help finding local programs.
